rules.name: Limitation of Liability Cap

standard_position.text: Each party's aggregate liability shall not exceed the total fees paid or payable in the 12 months preceding the claim. This limitation shall not apply to: (a) gross negligence or willful misconduct; (b) indemnification obligations; (c) breach of confidentiality; (d) infringement of intellectual property rights.

acceptable_deviations.text: Liability capped at 24 months of fees paid
acceptable_deviations.notes: Extended cap acceptable for high-value deals

acceptable_deviations.text: Liability capped at total contract value
Data breach carve-out up to 2x cap amount
Mutual cap with identical terms for both parties

unacceptable_deviations.text: No carve-out for gross negligence or willful misconduct

rules.guidance: LoL caps should reflect deal value and risk profile. Always preserve carve-outs for intentional conduct. Consider insurance coverage when negotiating cap amounts. Caps removing incentive to perform may be unenforceable under UCC 2-719.

legal_authority.holding: Consequential damage limitations are valid unless unconscionable

legal_authority.citation: Matter of Part 60 Put-Back Litig., 36 N.Y.3d 342 (2020)
legal_authority.holding: Limitation of liability clauses represent legitimate risk allocation and are enforceable unless gross negligence is excluded

legal_authority.citation: Sommer v. Fed. Signal Corp., 79 N.Y.2d 540 (1992)
legal_authority.holding: Gross negligence requires conduct that smacks of intentional wrongdoing or reckless indifference

US-NY.rule: Enforces unless gross negligence excluded; gross negligence must smack of intentional wrongdoing

US-CA.rule: Sliding-scale unconscionability analysis; both procedural AND substantive required but inversely weighted

US-PA.rule: 13 Pa.C.S. Section 2719 - commercial LoL clauses presumptively valid

US-IL.rule: Unenforceable if improvident, oppressive, or totally one-sided

US-CO.rule: Cannot limit liability for willful and wanton breach

rules.name: Termination Rights

standard_position.text: Either party may terminate: (a) for material breach with 30 days written notice and opportunity to cure; (b) immediately upon insolvency, bankruptcy, or assignment for benefit of creditors. Customer may terminate for convenience with 60 days notice. Vendor may terminate for convenience with 90 days notice. Upon termination: fees earned are due, data is returned/destroyed, and survival clauses remain effective.

acceptable_deviations.notes: Shorter cure acceptable for clear breaches

acceptable_deviations.text: Termination for convenience with early termination fee
acceptable_deviations.notes: Acceptable if fee is reasonable (e.g., 3 months fees)
Extended notice acceptable for complex transitions

unacceptable_deviations.text: Termination for convenience by counterparty only
No cure period for material breach

rules.guidance: Ensure balanced termination rights. Always preserve cure periods for breach. Termination for convenience provides flexibility but may require reasonable notice or wind-down fees. Post-termination obligations (data return, survival clauses) are critical.

legal_authority.holding: Material breach is determined by examining significance of breach, likelihood of cure, adequacy of compensation, and hardship

legal_authority.citation: K-Line Builders, Inc. v. First Fed. Sav. & Loan Ass'n, 677 S.W.2d 886 (Mo. 1984)
legal_authority.holding: Termination clauses allowing for cure are favored; immediate termination without cure may be unconscionable

rules.name: Data Protection and Privacy

standard_position.text: Vendor shall process Customer Data only as instructed by Customer and in accordance with applicable data protection laws (including GDPR, CCPA). Vendor implements appropriate technical and organizational measures (encryption, access controls, audit logging). Vendor will notify Customer of data breaches within 72 hours. Upon termination, Vendor will return or securely delete Customer Data. Subprocessors require prior written approval.

acceptable_deviations.text: Pre-approved list of subprocessors with notification of changes
Annual security audit with SOC 2 Type II report
Data retention for 30 days post-termination for transition

unacceptable_deviations.text: Vendor may use Customer Data for own purposes
No data deletion obligation upon termination

rules.guidance: Data protection is regulatory-driven and non-negotiable in many respects. GDPR requires 72-hour breach notification. CCPA requires service provider restrictions. Always preserve audit rights, breach notification, and deletion obligations.

legal_authority.holding: Processors must provide sufficient guarantees, process only on documented instructions, and assist with compliance obligations
Service providers must not retain, use, or disclose personal information except as necessary to perform services
Personal data breaches must be notified to supervisory authority within 72 hours

scoring_workflow.description: Identify clause type using NLP classification
Extract numerical values, durations, percentages, named entities
Compare extracted terms against standard_position and deviations
Compute final score: base_score x jurisdiction_modifier
Route based on score thresholds and auto_negotiate flag

training_data_schema.description: Schema for labeled training examples to improve clause classification and position matching

example_format.clause_text: Vendor's total aggregate liability shall not exceed the fees paid in the preceding six (6) months.
